handles: creating → assign family_id (either explicit from a rotation OR fresh UUID for a first-in-chain refresh)
creating → hash the plaintext secret via SHA-256 into token_hash; NEVER persist plaintext
creating → hash IP + user_agent via SHA-256 into ip_address_hash + user_agent_hash (plaintext never stored)

updating (consumed_at transition NULL → non-null) → refuse if row already has consumed_at set (that's the reuse-detection case; the RefreshAction path fires RefreshTokenReuseDetected + invalidates the family + returns 401 — the observer here is a defense-in-depth line that would catch a direct-DB write bug)
updating (consumed_at) → fire RefreshTokenRotated when the caller has already inserted the successor row in the same family (else this is a lone consume with no rotation, e.g. logout)
updating (revoked_at transition NULL → non-null) → fire SessionRevoked with the resolved reason from the row's metadata (set by the RevokeSessionAction)
deleting → refuse hard-delete when the row is still active (would break audit trail); soft-delete is fine
restoring → refuse (revoked refresh tokens must never be restored)
notes: The reuse-detection PATH is: RefreshAction receives a token, hashes it, looks up the row. If consumed_at IS NOT NULL, the request is a REPLAY — fire RefreshTokenReuseDetected + LockdownOnBreachDetected listener chain. This observer's `refuse if already consumed` branch is defensive — it catches the (rare) case where a caller bypasses RefreshAction and tries to UPDATE consumed_at directly.

family_id assignment: RefreshAction sets `family_id = old_refresh.family_id` when rotating; a first-time login sets family_id = fresh UUID. The observer preserves the value if the caller set it, else populates fresh.

handles: creating → refuse when the abilities compact array is missing the `_app` pseudo-ability (auth.enforce_session_scope depends on it)
creating → refuse when the abilities array is empty (a session with zero permissions is a bug — at minimum the `authenticated` ability is present)

updating (last_used_at) → update async via a batched queue job to reduce write pressure. Direct writes on every request would saturate the postgres row-lock queue under load. Instead, the auth guard emits a fire-and-forget UpdateLastUsedAtJob per (token_id, timestamp) that batches every 30 seconds and issues an UPDATE covering all touched tokens.
updating (revoked_at transition NULL → non-null) → fire SessionRevoked event with the resolved reason
deleting → also delete the paired refresh token in the same transaction (safety net; the auth actions should always cascade explicitly)
notes: Sanctum's shipped model doesn't know about the `_app` scope — our observer is what enforces the auth.enforce_session_scope contract at write-time.

Async last_used_at is a performance optimization — for a service handling 10K requests/second, updating every PAT synchronously would saturate the DB. The batched update is idempotent (LAST-write-wins is fine here; a few seconds of drift on last_used_at is acceptable).
